title-group.article-title: Ocular injuries following the pager explosion mass casualty event in Lebanon: a large single-centre case series

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Ophthalmology, American University of Beirut Medical Center, Beirut, Lebanon

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © Author(s) (or their employer(s)) 2026. Re-use permitted under CC BY-NC. No commercial re-use. See rights and permissions. Published by BMJ Group.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed in accordance with the Creative Commons Attribution Non Commercial (CC BY-NC 4.0) license, which permits others to distribute, remix, adapt, build upon this work non-commercially, and license their derivative works on different terms, provided the original work is properly cited, appropriate credit is given, any changes made indicated, and the use is non-commercial. See: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Background/Aims
sec.p: On 17 September 2024, over 3000 pager devices containing explosives were remotely detonated across Lebanon in a coordinated mass-casualty event, causing unprecedented ocular trauma. This study aims to characterise the nature and extent of ophthalmic injuries treated at a tertiary care centre following this unique attack.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A retrospective chart review was conducted of patients presenting to the American University of Beirut Medical Centre on the day of the explosion. Only patients who underwent ophthalmic surgical exploration were included. Data were collected from clinical notes, imaging and operative reports. Injuries were categorised using the Birmingham Eye Trauma Terminology (BETT) classification, and descriptive statistics were applied.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: 79 patients (158 eyes) underwent surgical exploration. Severe globe trauma was identified in 111 eyes (70.3%), including 61 eyes (55%) with open globe injuries and 50 (45%) with traumatic eviscerations. Bilateral injuries were noted in 45.6% of patients. Intraocular foreign bodies were confirmed in all traumatic eviscerations and 81.9% of open globe injuries. Evisceration was performed in 49 eyes (31%). Adnexal injuries requiring reconstruction occurred in 27.3% of eyes. Among eyes that underwent globe-sparing repair, 71.7% had postoperative visual acuity of no light perception or light perception.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This case series describes an unprecedented volume and severity of ocular injuries from a novel blast mechanism. The combination of thermal, chemical and penetrating trauma resulted in high rates of globe loss and poor visual outcomes. Preparedness for future unconventional mass-casualty scenarios must include rapid imaging, specialised ophthalmic teams and coordinated multidisciplinary care.

sec.title: WHAT IS ALREADY KNOWN ON THIS TOPIC

list-item.p: Blast-related ocular injuries are a leading cause of vision loss in mass-casualty events, but no prior reports describe trauma from coordinated detonations of handheld communication devices.

This study documents an unprecedented mechanism of ocular injury from pager detonations, showing exceptionally high rates of bilateral globe rupture, evisceration and intraocular foreign bodies, with poor visual outcomes.

sec.title: HOW THIS STUDY MIGHT AFFECT RESEARCH, PRACTICE, OR POLICY

list-item.p: Findings underscore the need for ophthalmic disaster preparedness, emphasising rapid imaging, specialised surgical teams and multidisciplinary coordination for future unconventional blast events.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Blast injuries are among the leading causes of vision-threatening trauma worldwide. The eye’s anatomic exposure and limited protective structures render it particularly susceptible to primary and secondary blast effects. Ocular injuries have been reported in 3–28% of victims across major mass-casualty incidents, including industrial explosions, terrorist attacks and improvised explosive device (IED) detonations.1 Previous reports of terror-related and improvised-explosive-device ocular trauma from military and civilian mass-casualty events, such as those seen in Iraq, Turkey and the Boston Marathon bombing, have described complex, multilayered ocular and adnexal injuries caused by high-velocity fragments and blast overpressure. These studies underscore the eye’s exceptional vulnerability to explosive forces and the devastating impact of close-range detonations on visual outcomes.26 On 17 September 2024, thousands of pager devices were synchronously detonated across Lebanon. This marked the first ever use of mass communication devices as a military target at this scale.7 Each device contained approximately 3–6 g of pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN) explosives that were triggered to explode on receipt of a synchronous pager message.8 9 According to local authorities, around 3000 individuals sustained severe injuries, with around two-thirds involving trauma to their faces, eyes and/or extremities.7

In this study, we report our tertiary centre’s experience managing one of the largest documented cohorts of ocular trauma arising from a mass-casualty event, characterising the spectrum and pattern of injuries that presented to the Emergency Department following this unprecedented attack.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This retrospective single-centre study reviewed medical records of patients presenting to the ED at the American University of Beirut Medical Centre (AUBMC) on 17 September 17, with ocular or orbital injuries related to the pager explosion mass-casualty event. The study was approved by the American University of Beirut Institutional Review Board (IRB protocol BIO-2024–0353) and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. A waiver of patient consent was granted due to the minimal risk posed to participants.

Following the incident, 177 patients presented to the ED, prompting activation of ‘Code D’, AUBMC’s institutional disaster response plan for mass-casualty incidents.10 On activation, all essential hospital staff were notified through paging, SMS and WhatsApp, and instructed to report to their respective departments. The plan enabled rapid mobilisation of multidisciplinary surgical teams, expansion of triage capacity beyond the Emergency Department into adjacent clinical areas, and centralised coordination of operating room allocation through the hospital’s command centre to manage the influx of casualties efficiently. Patient evacuation to AUBMC occurred through multiple routes, reflecting the overwhelmed state of emergency services during the incident. Some individuals were transported by Lebanese Red Cross and Civil Defence ambulances, while others arrived by private vehicles driven by relatives or bystanders.

All patients with suspected ocular injury were screened by the ophthalmology service in the emergency department and lower-acuity triage areas through bedside evaluation, including gross inspection, portable slit-lamp examination, fluorescein staining and CT of the orbits. Critically ill patients were rushed for urgent life-saving surgeries and were assessed either intraoperatively, in the post-anaesthesia care unit (PACU), or the following day in the ward.

The ophthalmology service comprised 12 faculty members and 7 residents, including subspecialists in cornea (n=4), oculoplastics (n=2), vitreoretinal surgery (n=2), glaucoma (n=1), medical retina (n=2), and neuro-ophthalmology (n=1). Faculty and residents provided 16-hour daily coverage, distributed across multiple operating rooms for surgical interventions and hospital wards for screening, triage and post-operative evaluation. Most patients sustained multiple systemic injuries. Surgical decisions, whether performed solely by the ophthalmology team or jointly with other specialties such as neurosurgery, plastic surgery, orthopaedics, urology or otolaryngology, depended on the type and severity of injury, logistical constraints and surgeon availability. When multidisciplinary coordination was not feasible, the ophthalmology service performed independent procedures addressing the most urgent ocular pathologies. Surgical prioritisation favoured open-globe injuries with retained light perception, while eyes with no light perception and irreparable anatomy were scheduled for evisceration at a lower priority.

All operated patients were reassessed the following day during postoperative rounds to monitor recovery and identify the need for additional interventions. Patients undergoing open-globe repair received topical and oral moxifloxacin, in addition to topical prednisolone acetate. Those undergoing evisceration received topical prednisolone acetate, and tetracycline ointment was applied to all eyelid lacerations.

Many repaired open-globe eyes exhibited indications for secondary procedures such as penetrating keratoplasty (PKP), vitreous haemorrhage (VH) clearance, rhegmatogenous retinal detachment (RRD) repair or lensectomy. These were deferred during the acute response phase due to resource limitations and the need to prioritise life-saving and vision-saving interventions. Following initial stabilisation, all patients were transferred to other facilities where staged procedures could be re-evaluated and performed as appropriate.

Due to the extreme patient influx and limited documentation capacity during this mass-casualty situation, comprehensive data were available only for those who underwent ophthalmic surgical exploration. Consequently, this study focuses on surgically managed cases with complete operative and imaging records. Patients with non-surgical ocular trauma—such as corneal foreign bodies, superficial burns or minor eyelid lacerations—were excluded due to incomplete documentation.

Using the subset of records in the electronic health record system (AUBHealth EPIC) for the 177 patients presenting with blast-related injuries, we identified all patients who underwent ophthalmic surgical exploration. Data were extracted from emergency records, operative notes and clinical progress documentation. Collected variables included patient demographics (age, gender), laterality of injury, anterior and posterior segment findings, orbital and facial examination results, surgical details (procedure type and timing), imaging findings and immediate postoperative outcomes. The period of record review covered the inpatient stay from 17 September to 22 September 2024, after which all patients were transferred to other facilities for continued care.

To categorise and analyse the ocular injuries systematically, we applied the Birmingham Eye Trauma Terminology (BETT) classification system.11 BETT distinguishes closed globe injuries, in which the eyewall remains intact, from open globe injuries, characterised by full-thickness lacerations. Since the BETT classification does not define eyes exhibiting extensive tissue loss and damage beyond anatomic repair, these eyes were classified as cases of traumatic evisceration. Intraocular foreign bodies (IOFBs) were identified by direct intraoperative visualisation or confirmed via CT imaging.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: This study used a descriptive analytical approach, with no inferential statistical testing performed due to the observational and retrospective design. Continuous variables are reported as means with standard errors (SE), while categorical variables are summarised using frequencies and percentages. Data were managed and analysed using the SPSS statistical package version 29.0 for Windows (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). Missing data were handled via listwise deletion for each variable analysed.

sec.title: Patient demographics and injury overview
sec.p: Following the mass-casualty event, a total of 177 patients simultaneously presented to the ED, with at least 98 sustaining ocular traumas. Surgical exploration was performed by the ophthalmology service on 79 patients (158 eyes).

The mean age of patients surgically explored was 34.9±9.6 years (range, 20–59 years; median, 33 years), with men comprising 96% of the cohort. Orbital CT imaging was obtained in 75 patients (95%), and the majority (90.6%) were performed preoperatively. Among those surgically explored, 11 (14%) patients required a second surgical intervention for previously deferred procedures, resulting in a total of 90 ophthalmic surgeries over a span of 4 days.

Surgical interventions were performed by a team of 12 ophthalmology faculty members across various subspecialties, supported by 4 ophthalmology residents. More than half of the surgeries (54.5%) were completed within the first 32 hours post-event. The surgeries were distributed over 4 days as follows: 15.6% on the first night, 38.9% on the second day, 32.2% on the third day, and 13.3% on the fourth day.

sec.title: Distribution of ocular injuries
sec.p: Table 1 summarises the distribution of patient-level ocular injuries. Of the 79 patients surgically explored, 75 (94.9%) sustained severe globe injuries (either a case of open globe injury or traumatic evisceration) in at least one eye. Among patients suffering from severe globe injuries, 39 (52%) had unilateral involvement, while 36 (48%) sustained bilateral injuries.

Figure 1 illustrates the distribution of eye-level ocular injuries within the cohort. Severe globe trauma was identified in 111 eyes (70.3%), including 61 eyes (55%) with open globe injuries (figure 2A, B) and 50 eyes (45%) with traumatic eviscerations (figure 2C, D). The left eye was more commonly affected, representing 62 injuries (55.9%). All 50 (100%) eyes that suffered from traumatic eviscerations demonstrated extensive fragmentation with embedded IOFBs, which were retrieved from globe remnants, the orbit and adjacent adnexal tissues (figures 3 and 4). CT imaging confirmed the presence of IOFBs in 50 of 61 eyes (81.9%) with open globe injuries (figure 4 - Missing).

caption.title: Distribution of ocular injuries and surgical interventions in patients following the pager explosion.

Case examples of severe globe trauma patterns following pager explosion injuries. (A, B) Eyes with traumatic evisceration demonstrating grossly distorted anatomy containing foreign bodies. (C, D) Open globe injuries with full-thickness scleral and corneal lacerations.

Case examples of retrieved intraocular foreign bodies (IOFBs) from injured eyes following pager explosion. (A) Plastic and metallic fragments from pager casing; (B) Glass shard from pager display; (C) Internal metallic component; (D) Electronic chip fragment.

CT imaging and 3D reconstruction of a case example demonstrating multiple intraorbital and facial foreign bodies from pager explosion injury. CT demonstrates comminuted fractures of the bilateral maxillary sinuses, nasal bones, left anterior ethmoid air cells, anterior wall of the left frontal sinus and bilateral orbital floors with associated hemosinus, as well as fractures of the left lamina papyracea and left lateral orbital wall with small bony fragments in the left superior extraconal space. Bilateral open globe injuries are evident, with deformed globe contours, vitreous haemorrhage, intraocular and periocular gas and multiple intraocular and intraorbital radiodense foreign bodies. Numerous additional superficial foreign bodies are embedded within the periorbital soft tissues and anterior facial region.

sec.title: Surgical interventions
sec.p: Among the 61 eyes with open globe injuries, 8 (13.1%) underwent surgical exploration without requiring surgical intervention due to self-sealing scleral/corneal wounds or absence of full-thickness injury. These injuries had been identified on CT imaging as open globes due to the presence of foreign bodies within the vitreous cavity. The remaining 53 eyes (86.9%) required full-thickness laceration repair or suturing. Full-thickness lacerations were distributed as follows: 7 eyes (13.2%) had scleral lacerations, 18 eyes (34%) had corneal lacerations and 28 eyes (52.8%) had corneoscleral lacerations.

Of the 50 globes with traumatic evisceration, 49 (98%) underwent surgical evisceration, with 47 eyes (96%) receiving an orbital implant during the same surgical session. One evisceration (1.8%) was cancelled at the patient’s request. Overall, the total evisceration rate was 31% (49 of 158 eyes).

All patients exhibited varying degrees of facial and eyelid burns, skin necrosis and superficial abrasions and lacerations (figure 5). Severe ocular adnexal injuries (figure 6) requiring surgical reconstruction were identified in 43 of 158 eyes (27.3%), of which 39 underwent primary eyelid reconstruction, while treatment was deferred in four eyes. Among the reconstructed eyelids, 12 cases (30.8%) would require further secondary reconstructive procedures to optimise functional and cosmetic outcomes.

caption.title: Case examples of thermal and chemical burns, lacerations and superficial tissue injuries of the face and ocular adnexa following pager explosion.
Case examples of severe ocular adnexal injuries requiring surgical reconstruction after pager explosion.

sec.title: Postoperative outcomes
sec.p: The distribution of postoperative visual acuity (VA) outcomes at the patient level is summarised in table 2. Visual acuity was measured bedside on postoperative day 1 or 2 using uncorrected visual acuity (UCVA). The period of record review extended through 22 September 2024, after which all patients were transferred to other facilities for continued care. As such, these inpatient assessments represent the final available postoperative visual data. Among the 58 eyes with intact globes or no evidence of open globe injury, immediate inpatient postoperative assessment revealed that one eye (1.7%) had no light perception (NLP), 1 eye (1.7%) retained light perception (LP), 4 eyes (6.9%) could detect hand motion (HM), and 52 eyes (89.7%) achieved visual acuity of counting fingers (CF) or better. Of the eight eyes with open globe injuries not requiring laceration repair, three (37.5%) demonstrated hand motion vision, while five (62.5%) achieved counting fingers vision or better. In the subset of 53 eyes that underwent full-thickness laceration repair, bedside visual acuity could not be assessed in seven eyes due to patient transfer prior to postoperative evaluation the following day. Among the 46 eyes assessed, 12 eyes (26.1%) had NLP, 21 (45.6%) LP, 9 (19.6%) demonstrated HM, and 4 (8.7%) were able to CF or better.

tr.th: Visual Acuity in the better eye

fn.p: Postoperative visual acuity evaluation data available only for 72 patients.
CF, counting fingers; HM, hand motion; LP, light perception; NLP, no light perception.

sec.p: Postoperative complications were assessed during the inpatient observation period (48–72 hours). No clinical evidence of postoperative infection was identified in eviscerated eyes, and no cases of early endophthalmitis were observed among the open-globe cohort.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This retrospective analysis of ocular injuries sustained during the pager explosion incident reveals an unprecedented prevalence of ocular trauma in any mass casualty event. Among all the trauma patients presenting to the ED, 55.4% had ocular injuries and 44.6% underwent examination under anaesthesia.

To our knowledge, this represents one of the largest documented single-centre cohorts of ocular trauma resulting from an unconventional mass-casualty event: the simultaneous detonation of thousands of handheld pagers distributed across multiple locations in Lebanon. According to the Ministry of Health, approximately 2800 injuries were reported overall, including about 1850 in Beirut, 750 in southern Lebanon and 150 in the Bekaa Valley.12 Within this context, the American University of Beirut Medical Centre (AUBMC) received 177 patients with blast-related trauma, representing the largest single-centre cohort in the country. The unique mechanism of injury, occurring at the moment the user activated the pager, produced close-range facial blasts with radially projected high-velocity fragments. This accounts for the predominance of ocular and hand injuries. Consequently, 94.9% of surgically explored patients sustained severe globe injury in at least one eye, and nearly half (45.6%) demonstrated bilateral involvement. The design and composition of the devices directly contributed to the injury pattern. Embedded components, including lithium batteries, circuit boards and metal fragments, served as sources of mechanical, thermal and chemical injury on detonation.8 IOFBs were identified in all eyes with traumatic evisceration and in 81.9% of open globe injuries, most commonly consisting of pager-derived plastic, metal or ceramic debris. The involvement of lithium batteries likely intensified periorbital and adnexal tissue loss via thermal burns, chemical exposure and hydrogen fluoride release, compounding the extent of soft tissue necrosis and complicating reconstructive efforts.13 14

The pattern and severity of ocular injuries observed in this cohort are unprecedented. Severe globe injuries accounted for 70.3% of surgically explored eyes and 57.8% of all eyes with ocular trauma, rates far exceeding those reported after the 2020 Beirut Port explosion (20.8%), the 2015 Tianjin industrial blast (13.2%), and the 1995 Oklahoma City bombing (21.8%).1517 The evisceration rate in this series (31%) likewise surpassed those of these events (Beirut Port: 9.6%; Tianjin: 9.4%; Oklahoma: 16.4%) and approached levels documented in military combat ocular trauma.1517 During the Iraq War, up to 79% of eyes exposed to explosive fragments sustained open-globe injury, with 16–18% requiring enucleation or evisceration.5 6 Similarly, Erdurman et al observed that 79% of ocular injuries from improvised explosive devices were open-globe injuries, with 28% of those eyes ultimately requiring removal, while Guven later reported long-term evisceration or enucleation in approximately 30% of terror-related open-globe injuries.3 4 This constellation of findings reflects the close-range and fragment-rich nature of the pager detonations, which produced extensive, often irreparable, ocular damage. The exceptionally high rate of bilateral globe injuries (45.6%) further distinguishes this event from prior explosions, where blast energy is typically dispersed and facial trauma less concentrated. In contrast, the direct and proximal detonation produced a combination of thermal, mechanical and chemical forces that resulted in profound adnexal tissue loss and frequent need for eyelid reconstruction and secondary procedures, underscoring the complexity of the required surgical management. Collectively, these parallels underscore that the ophthalmic devastation from the pager detonations equalled or exceeded that seen in conventional combat and terror-related blast injuries, despite occurring in a civilian setting.

Visual prognosis following blast-related ocular trauma remains predictably poor, consistent with the extent of injuries in this cohort. All eviscerated eyes presented with NLP at initial assessment. Among eyes that underwent globe-sparing repair, the majority (71.7%) demonstrated severely compromised postoperative vision (NLP or LP) paralleling outcomes reported in combat-related ocular trauma. Weichel et al reported that 63% of combat-related open globe injuries resulted in vision worse than 20/200, paralleling the severe outcomes observed in our cohort.18 In the Beirut Port explosion cohort, all four eyes presenting primary NLP ultimately required evisceration, reaffirming the strong association between initial NLP and irreversible ocular damage.17 Predictors of poor visual prognosis—initial VA, perforating injuries, globe rupture and retinal detachment were uniformly prevalent in our series, compounded by extensive orbital and adnexal trauma.19 CT was indispensable for surgical planning, allowing precise localisation of IOFBs and evaluation of orbital fractures and craniofacial injuries. Rapid, high-resolution imaging enabled decisions regarding globe-sparing repair versus evisceration and guided coordination with otolaryngology, plastic surgery, vascular surgery and orthopaedic surgery teams. This aligns with established recommendations identifying CT as the imaging modality of choice for blast-related ocular trauma.1 The triage strategy, emphasising rapid assessment, prioritisation of salvageable globes and real-time multidisciplinary coordination, was critical in managing the overwhelming case volume. Together, these measures highlight essential principles for mass-casualty preparedness: the need for immediate access to advanced imaging, streamlined triage and integrated surgical teams, particularly vitreoretinal and oculoplastic specialists, as core components of effective disaster response. Future preparedness planning must integrate these experiences to anticipate novel blast scenarios and strengthen ophthalmic disaster planning.

Several limitations merit acknowledgement. The retrospective design and the chaotic context of a mass-casualty disaster limited data completeness, despite rigorous chart review. Patients with non-surgical ocular trauma were excluded due to incomplete documentation, potentially underestimating the overall spectrum of ocular trauma. Complications and long-term visual and reconstructive outcomes remain unknown, as patients were transferred to other facilities after initial stabilisation. The inability to apply the Ocular Trauma Score (OTS) due to incomplete data on key variables represents an additional limitation, hindering standardised injury severity assessment and comparison with other ocular trauma cohorts. While the unusual injury mechanism constrains generalisability, these findings offer valuable insights into emerging and unconventional warfare and its ophthalmologic consequences.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This series offers a rare, detailed account of the devastating ocular morbidity resulting from the coordinated detonation of handheld pagers, a previously unrecognised weapon. The combination of mechanical, thermal and chemical injury mechanisms produced globe and adnexal trauma at frequencies and severities surpassing prior blast events. Early CT imaging, comprehensive multidisciplinary surgical intervention and specialised reconstructive expertise were essential in managing this complex injury burden. As unconventional threats emerge, trauma systems and disaster response protocols must evolve to ensure readiness for increasingly sophisticated and devastating injury mechanisms.

fn.p: Funding: The authors have not declared a specific grant for this research from any funding agency in the public, commercial or not-for-profit sectors.
Patient consent for publication: Not applicable.
Provenance and peer review: Not commissioned; externally peer reviewed.

Ethics approval: This study was performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and with the approval for retrospective review of patient data by the Institutional Review Board at the American University of Beirut (IRB protocol BIO-2024-0353).

Correction notice: This paper has been corrected since it was first published. Following an internal review by the authors' institutional review board, some specific revisions were made to the manuscript for medicolegal compliance

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: All data relevant to the study are included in the article or uploaded as supplementary information.
